{{nihongo|'''Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha'''|富士山本宮浅間大社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Suruga Province]].

{{List of coordinates}}'''Fujisan Hongu Sengen Taisha'' is a {{ill|lt=Shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|qid=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Fujinomiya City|Q496072|qid=Q496072|1=Fujinomiya, Shizuoka}}. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinai Shrine|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} ({{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}), {{ill|lt=Suruga Province|Q864170|qid=Q864170|1=Suruga Province}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|qid=Q1656379|1=Ichinomiya}}. {{ill|lt=Modern Shrine Ranking System|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=Old Shrine Ranking|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|lt=Modern Shrine Ranking System|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=Old Shrine Ranking|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} is {{ill|lt=Modern Shrine Ranking System|Government Shrine|qid=Government Shrine|ltq=Q712534}} and {{ill|lt=Beppyo Shrine|Q10898274|qid=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Shinto Honcho|Q908077|qid=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}. {{ill|lt=Shake|Q11588367|qid=Q11588367|1=Shake (social class)}} is {{ill|lt=Mr. Fuji|Q11456091|qid=Q11456091|1=Fuji clan}}. It is the headquarters of {{ill|lt=Asama Shrine|Q3471785|qid=Q3471785|1=Sengen shrine}}.

The main shrine is named "Fujisan Hongu Sengen Taisha" as one of the constituent assets of "{{ill|lt=Mt. Fuji - Object of worship and source of art|Q16482294|qid=Q16482294|1=Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration}}", and is also known as the inner shrine at the top of {{ill|lt=Mt. Fuji (representative topics)|UNKNOWN|lt=Mt. Fuji}} and the {{ill|lt=Kusushi Shrine (Mt. Fuji)|Q11369789|qid=Q11369789|lt=Kusushi Shrine|1=Kusushi Shrine}} is registered as a {{ill|lt=Cultural Heritage (World Heritage)|Q210272|qid=Q210272|lt=World Cultural Heritage|1=Cultural heritage}} as part of the ``Mountaintop Faith Ruins'' that is part of the ``Mt. Fuji Area.''

=== Overview ===
[[File:Sakura and Mt. Fuji Sakura and Mt. Fuji.jpg|alt=Sacred Mountain Fuji|Thumbnail|195x195 pixels|Sacred Mountain Fuji]]
It is a {{ill|lt=shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} that enshrines {{ill|lt=Mt. Fuji (main topic)|UNKNOWN|lt=Mt.Fuji}} as {{ill|lt=Shintaiyama|Q1595289|qid=Q1595289|1=Sacred mountains}}, and the {{ill|lt=precincts|Q11429002|qid=Q11429002|1=keidai}} consists of two shrines: {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=below|1=UNKNOWN}} name="okunomiya">[http://www.mof.go.jp/about_mof/zaimu/30years/main/0303020103.htm Special issues regarding land disposal within the precincts [Mt. Fuji summit precincts] (Ministry of Finance HP)]</ref>.
* '''{{ill|lt=#Hongu|UNKNOWN|lt=Hongu}}''' - Southern foot of Mt. Fuji (Fujinomiya city area)
* '''{{ill|lt=#Okumiya|UNKNOWN|lt=Okumiya}}''' - Summit of Mt. FujiSengen Taisha is the headquarters of {{ill|lt=Sengen Shrines|Q3471785|qid=Q3471785|1=Sengen shrine}} across the country, and is known as the center of {{ill|lt=Fuji worship|Q11455920|qid=Q11455920|1=Fujisan Sengen Faith}}. The precincts are vast, with the main shrine site measuring approximately 17,000 m<sup>2</sup>, and the shrine also owns approximately 3.85 million m<sup>2</sup> above the 8th station of Mt. Fuji.<ref>``30 years after the government ruled that the summit of Mt. Fuji was transferred to a shrine in Shizuoka, the ownership has been decided and the prefectural border issue remains unresolved.'' (Yamanashi Nichinichi Shimbun) December 18, 2004)</ref>. The main hall of the main shrine was built by {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|qid=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} and has a unique shrine architectural style called ``{{ill|lt=Sengen-zukuri|Q11557481|qid=Q11557481|1=Sengen-zukuri}},'' and is designated as a national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|qid=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}. In addition, within the grounds of the main shrine, there is ``{{ill|lt=Wakutama Pond|Q11563392|qid=Q11563392|1=Wakutamaike}}'' where spring water from Mt. Fuji gushes out, and it has been designated as a national {{ill|lt=Natural Monument #Special Natural Monument|Q23790|qid=Q23790|lt=Special Natural Monument|1=Natural monument}}.The enshrined deity is {{ill|lt=Konohana no Sakuyabime|Q1781862|qid=Q1781862|lt=Konohananosakuyabime-no-mikoto|1=Konohanasakuya-hime}}, and the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=cherry blossom|1=UNKNOWN}} associated with the enshrined deity is considered a sacred tree, and about 500 cherry trees are dedicated within the precincts. In addition, since ancient times, {{ill|lt=Mr. Fuji|Q11456091|qid=Q11456091|1=Fuji clan}} has served as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Grand Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}, and is counted as one of Japan's three greatest chief priests.<ref group="Commentary">In the article dated June 18, 1967 in the Gaun Nikjiroku, there is a mention of ``Japan's so-called three major chief priests'', and the others include {{ill|lt=Itsukushima Shinto priest family|Q11410131|qid=Q11410131|1=Itsukushima kannushi family}} and {{ill|lt=Atsuta Daiguji family|Q131871612|qid=Q131871612|1=Atsuta Daiguji Family}} ({{ill|lt=Chiaki family|Q134930771|qid=Q134930771|1=Senshūke}}). </ref>. In addition to being deeply revered by the imperial court and the samurai class since ancient times, the shrine grounds are located at the starting point of the {{ill|lt=Omiya-Murayama-guchi Ascending Route|Q11455992|qid=Q11455992|1=Ōmiya-Murayama Ascending Route}}, and since ancient times it has also been revered by {{ill|lt=Shugendo|Q1483875|qid=Q1483875|lt=Shugenja|1=Shugendō}} who practice mountain climbing.

=== About the company name ===
In ancient times, it was listed as ``Sengen Shrine'' in the ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}},'' and in the Meiji period, the official name was ``Fujisan Hongu Sengen Shrine.'' From {{ill|lt=1982|Q2436|qid=Q2436|1=1982}} (1982), the current official name is ``Fujisan Hongu Sengen Taisha'', and the abbreviation ``''Sengen Taisha'''' is often used.

There are various theories about the origin of the word "Asama," but it is said to mean a volcano, such as {{ill|lt=Mt. Asama|Q242730|qid=Q242730|1=Mount Asama}} in Nagano Prefecture. </ref>. "Asama" is an old name, and the current "Sengen" is thought to have been used since {{ill|lt=Middle Ages|Q12554|qid=Q12554|1=Middle Ages}}. </ref>. Also, "Hongu" is the name for {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Sengen Shrine|Q500368|qid=Q500368|1=Shizuoka Sengen Shrine}} (Shingu) <ref name="Miyaji (1929) P21" />.In addition, in ancient times, "Fujinomiya," "Fuji Hongu," and "Fuji Sengengu" were also used as company names. </ref>. It is also called ``''Fuji no Miya'''', and {{ill|lt=Yasutoki Hojo|Q1188359|qid=Q1188359|1=Hōjō Yasutoki}} visited Sengen Shrine. group="hara">It is mentioned in the lyrics of a waka poem he wrote on March 26, 1219, in ``Azuma Kagami'' (included in ``{{ill|lt=New Imperial Selected Waka Collection|Q1143033|qid=Q1143033|1=Shinchokusen Wakashū}}'') This word is also the origin of the name of Fujinomiya City, where Sengen Taisha Shrine is located.

During the period of syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism, in addition to ``Fuji Gongen,'' it was also called ``Omiya Gongen'' and ``Fuji Sengen Gongen.''

=== Enshrined God ===
<!--The name of the enshrined deity is according to the official website-->
; Main enshrined deity
:* '''{{ill|lt=Konohana no Sakuyabime|Q1781862|qid=Q1781862|lt=Konohana no Sakuyabime no Mikoto|1=Konohanasakuya-hime}}''' (Konohana no Sakuyahime no Mikoto) <ref>[http://www.fuji-hongu.or.jp/sengen/history/index.html Enshrined Deity Official Website]</ref>
:** Another name is ``''Asama no Okami'' (Asama no Okami).
:** The divine name is also written as ``Konohana Sakuyahime no Mikoto'' in some historical books, but Sengen Taisha uses the notation listed in ``{{ill|lt=Kojiki|Q813031|qid=Q813031|1=Kojiki}}'' as its official name. ``Konoka'' is said to refer to cherry blossoms, and the book describes her as a beautiful woman and also includes a story about a woman giving birth in a fire.
; Goddess
:* '''{{jalink|Ninigi|Q1056017|Ninigi no Mikoto}''' (Ninigi no Mikoto) - The husband god of Konohananosaku Yabime no Mikoto.
:* '''{{ill|lt=Ooyamatsumi|Q386563|qid=Q386563|lt=Oyamazuminokami|1=Ōyamatsumi}}''' (Oyamazuminokami) - The father god of Konohananosaku Yabiume no Mikoto.

=== About the enshrined deity ===
[[File:Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha daitorii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Otorii and [[Mt. Fuji (typical topics)|Mt. Fuji]]}}]]
There has been a belief that Mt. Fuji is a goddess for a long time due to its beautiful appearance, and in {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|qid=Q193292|1=Heian period}}, "Mt. Fuji" written by {{ill|lt=Muraka|Q11643817|qid=Q11643817|1=Miyako no Yoshika}} was written. (included in ``{{ill|lt=Honcho Bunsui|Q60976|qid=Q60976|1=Honchō Monzui}}'') as ``Asama Okami,'' and in ``The Tale of the Bamboo Cutter'' as ``Kaguyahime.'' <ref> ``World Encyclopedia'' (Heibonsha) Mt. </ref>. However, it seems that Konohananosakuyahime, which appears in the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, was applied to this in the early modern period, and until then, it was generally worshiped under the name ``Asama no Kami.''The old name for "Asama", "Asama", is considered to be a name representing "volcano", as seen in {{ill|lt=Mt. Aso|Q733710|qid=Q733710|1=Mount Aso}}, {{ill|lt=Mt. Asama|Q242730|qid=Q242730|1=Mount Asama}}, {{ill|lt=Mt. Asahidake (Tochigi Prefecture)|Q11517490|qid=Q11517490|lt=Dake Asahidake|1=Mount Asahi}}.<ref name="Nomoto P210" /> Tsurakura's description also mentions the eruption in {{ill|lt=Enryaku|Q1373176|qid=Q1373176|1=Enryaku}}21 ({{ill|lt=802|Q29992|qid=Q29992|1=802}}), and it is thought that the name ``Sengen God'' was born around this time. </ref>. In the {{ill|lt=Shinto-Buddhist syncretism|Q2279088|qid=Q2279088|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} period after the Middle Ages, it was also called ``Fuji Daibosatsu'', ``Asama Daibosatsu'', and further down, ``Fuji Gongen''<ref name="Shizuoka Prefecture" />.The origin of the apotheosis of the divine spirit of Mt. Fuji to Konohananosakuyahime is not clear, but the first reference to the document is the ``Shuun Osho Shimano'' from the early {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}}<ref name="History of Fuji">P22-29 of ``History of Fuji'' edited by Sengen Shrine. </ref>. "Konohana" (wooden flower) is said to be the old name for cherry blossoms, and the enshrined deity is said to be derived from the beauty of Mt. Fuji <ref>宮地直一、広野三郎 著、浅間神社 編『浅間神社の歴史』今古書院〈富士の研究〉、1929年。P11</ref>. Additionally, the myth of ``{{ill|lt=Descent of the Son of Heaven|Q1084733|qid=Q1084733|lt=Konohananosakuyahime's birth in the fire|1=Tenson kōrin}}'' seems to have been considered as an event related to fire. Also, the enshrined deity of Mishima Shrine ({{ill|lt=Mishima Taisha|Q650772|qid=Q650772|1=Mishima Taisha}}) is considered to be {{ill|lt=Oyamatsumi|Q386563|qid=Q386563|lt=Oyamazumi|1=Ōyamatsumi}}, and legends that Fuji and Mishima are father and son have been seen here and there since the Edo period<ref name="History of Fuji" />.

In the Edo period ``Kokin Yodanko'' by {{ill|lt=Yashiro Hiroken|Q6538130|qid=Q6538130|1=Yashiro Hirokata}}, there is an expression that ``two gods are enshrined,'' but now, as mentioned above, ``Asama no Okami is another name for Konohananosakuyabime no mikoto,'' and one syncretized deity is the main deity enshrined. Regarding the deities enshrined, the "Fuji Hongu Sengenshaki" describes the deities as Taigensonjin and Oyamazumijin. Taigen deity is said to be {{ill|lt=Kuni no Tokotachi deity|Q478504|qid=Q478504|lt=Kuni Tokodate deity|1=Kuni-no-Tokotachi}}, but from the first year of the Meiji era until the present, instead of Taigen deity, Keijoki no Mikoto was one of the deities enshrined <ref>宮地直一、広野三郎 著、浅間神社 編『浅間神社の歴史』今古書院〈富士の研究〉、1929年。P4</ref>.

</div></div>[[File:Yamamiya sengen1.JPG|thumb|180px|right|{{center|{{ill|lt=Yamamiya Sengen Shrine|Q11467749|qid=Q11467749|1=Yamamiya Sengen Shrine}}}}{{small|The main shrine of Sengen Taisha. }}]]
[[File:Fukuchi-jinja haiden-1.JPG|thumb|180px|right|{{center|{{ill|lt=Fuchi Shrine|Q11457275|qid=Q11457275|1=Fukuchi Shrine}}}}{{small|A landowner deity that was enshrined before the transfer of Sengen Taisha Shrine. }}]]The history of Sengen Taisha can be traced to the shrine biography "Fuji Hongu Sengen" written by the chief priest Fuji Tamizai during the {{ill|lt=Kansei|Q1195131|qid=Q1195131|1=Kansei}} years ({{ill|lt=1789|Q7772|qid=Q7772|1=1789}}-{{ill|lt=1801|Q6877|qid=Q6877|1=1801}}). <ref>{{ill|lt=#Endo (1987)|UNKNOWN|lt=Endo (1987)}}P8-11</ref><ref>宮地直一、広野三郎 著、浅間神社 編『浅間神社の歴史』今古書院〈富士の研究〉、1929年。P18</ref><ref group="Notes">Partially published in "Mt. Fuji Faith and Fujizuka" edited by Fuji City Museum, p.7. </ref>. According to the same article, it was enshrined at the foot of the mountain at the foot of Mt. Fuji in the year {{ill|lt=Emperor Suinin|Q314850|qid=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}. During the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Keiko|Q329723|qid=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}, when {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nihon Takeru|1=UNKNOWN}} was attacked by bandits in Suruga Province and encountered a wildfire disaster, he prayed to Asama no Okami and escaped the disaster. {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Iwakai|1=UNKNOWN}} was established at {{ill|lt=Yamamiya Sengen Shrine|Q11467749|qid=Q11467749|1=Yamamiya Sengen Shrine}}) and Asama Okami was enshrined there. Later, in the first year of {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|qid=Q1373191|lt=Daido|1=Daidō}} ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|qid=Q30021|1=806}}), it is said that {{ill|lt=Sakanoue Tamuramaro|Q941979|qid=Q941979|1=Sakanoue no Tamuramaro}} built a shrine on the current site of Omiya, by order of {{ill|lt=Emperor Heijo|Q353558|qid=Q353558|1=Emperor Heizei}}. According to the same article, the land of Omiya was originally the shrine of ``Fukuchijin,'' but when Sengenjin moved from Yamamiya, this place was also relocated (currently known as {{ill|lt=Fuchi Shrine|Q11457275|qid=Q11457275|1=Fukuchi Shrine}}).On the other hand, the first official history of Mt. Fuji's eruption is "{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihongi|Q611399|qid=Q611399|1=Shoku Nihongi}}". (Japan)|Q1373125|Ten'ō}} July 1st year ({{ill|lt=781|Q31175|qid=Q31175|1=781}}), and before then it was only described as a calm mountain and no eruptions were believed to have occurred <ref>{{ill|lt=#Nomoto (1987)|UNKNOWN|lt=Nomoto (1987)}}P208</ref>. The deity of "Asama-kami" is also {{ill|lt=flame worship|Q2638573|qid=Q2638573|lt=god of fire|1=Fire worship}}, and the year 3 ({{ill|lt=853 After the investiture of Jusanmi (Junior Third Rank|Q11591025|qid=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} in 2003|Q30367}}) (the first appearance of a divine name in the literature), in conjunction with the eruption of Mt. Fuji, the ascension to the divine rank for fire suppression has also been confirmed.<ref name="Nomoto P210" />. For these reasons, there is a necessity for the nation to enshrine the Sengen deity in order to extinguish the fires of Mt. Fuji, and it is thought that the actual enshrinement took place from the time the eruption occurred until the deity was relocated, that is, "between the first year of Tenno (781) and the first year of Daido (806)"<ref name="P210 Nomoto" />.Additionally, Tomi Shrine, which was originally enshrined in Omiya, is currently enshrined in the northern part of the main shrine grounds, and has been deeply involved in the rituals of Sengen Taisha Shrine since ancient times as the {{ill|lt=landlord|Q4160872|qid=Q4160872|1=Landlord deity}} of Omiya. In addition to the fact that the divine name of "Fuchi" is thought to be closely related to the mountain name of "Fuji," it is also believed that Mt. Fuji was worshiped as a deity of {{ill|lt=Water God|Q3137449|qid=Q3137449|1=Suijin}}, with {{ill|lt=Wakutama Pond|Q11563392|qid=Q11563392|1=Wakutamaike}} as a ritual site.<ref name="Nomoto P210">{{ill|lt=#Nomoto (1987)|UNKNOWN|lt=Nomoto (1987)}}P210</ref>. For this reason, the transfer of Asama no Kami is understood to be a symbolic event that represents the change in the worship of Fuji from ``Fukuchi Fuji,'' the god of water, to ``Asama,'' the god of fire<ref name="Nomoto P210" />.

=== Outline ===

==== Heian period ====
In {{ill|lt=Rokukokushi|Q1151168|qid=Q1151168|1=Rikkokushi}}, in {{ill|lt=Jinju|Q1373077|qid=Q1373077|1=Ninju}} 3rd year ({{ill|lt=853|Q30367|qid=Q30367|1=853}}), {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha #Meishin Name|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|lt=Meishin|1=Myōjin Taisha}}<ref group="original">``Japanese Emperor Montoku's Annals'' July 5, 3rd year of Ninju ([http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/120101.html Sengen Shrine]<Shinto/Shrine Historical Materials Collection>). </ref>・Junior Third Rank<ref group="Hara"> "Japanese Emperor Montoku Annals" article dated July 13, 3rd year of Ninju ([http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/120101.html Sengen Shrine]<Shinto/Shrine Historical Materials Collection>). </ref>. Although this is also the first mention of ``Asama no Kami'', it is difficult to imagine that he was given the high rank of Jusanmi from the beginning, and the formation of the divine name seems to date back even further. </ref>. {{ill|lt=Jōkan (Japan)|Q1075635|qid=Q1075635|lt=Jōkan|1=Jōgan}} In the first year of the year ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|qid=Q23897|1=859}}), he was awarded the Shosanmi rank. Sengen Shrine]<Shinto/Shrine Historical Materials Collection>). </ref>.In addition, regarding the {{ill|lt=Jogan Great Eruption|Q11634546|qid=Q11634546|1=Jōgan eruption of Mount Fuji}} of Mt. Fuji, which caused a lot of damage from the 6th year of Jogan ({{ill|lt=864|Q25041|qid=Q25041|1=864}}) to the 8th year of Jogan ({{ill|lt=866|Q23872|qid=Q23872|1=866}}), the Imperial Court used fortune-telling to attribute the eruption to the neglect of rituals at Sengen Shrine.<ref group="Hara">``Nihon Sandai Jiroku,'' August 5, 1987, article ([http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/120101.html Sengen Shrine]<Shinto/Shrine Historical Materials Collection>). </ref>. As a result, the Sengen-kami was enshrined in Kai Province as well.<ref group="Hara">``Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku,'' article dated December 9, 1987 ([http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/120101.html Sengen Shrine] <Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection>). </ref>As a result, the Asama faith spread to the Kai side as well.

[[File:Shizuoka-sengen-jinja ohaiden.JPG|thumb|180px|right|{{center|{{ill|lt=Shizuoka Sengen Shrine|Q500368|qid=Q500368|1=Shizuoka Sengen Shrine}}}}{{small|"Shingu" was commissioned from Sengen Taisha Shrine to Suruga Kokufu. }}]]Since then, it has been revered by the imperial court, and in the ``{{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'' it is listed as ``Suruga Province{{ill|lt=Fuji County|Q1154962|qid=Q1154962|1=Fuji District, Shizuoka}} Sengen Shrine Meishindai'' and is listed as {{ill|lt=Meishin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}. It was also revered as the Ichinomiya of Suruga Province. Sengen Shrine (currently one of the {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Sengen Shrines|Q500368|qid=Q500368|1=Shizuoka Sengen Shrine}}) was also built near Suruga Kokufu at the request of Sengen Taisha. In contrast to Sengen Taisha Shrine, which is the main shrine, it is called the Shingu Shrine. In addition, Sengen Shrine in Kai Province is also the only famous shrine in the country, and Ronsha is {{ill|lt=Sengen Shrine (Fuefuki City)|Q11557476|qid=Q11557476|lt=Sengen Shrine|1=Ichinomiya Asama Shrine (Fuefuki)}} ({{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|qid=Q132720|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Fuefuki City|Q849434|qid=Q849434|1=Fuefuki}}), {{ill|lt=Kawaguchi Sengen Shrine|Q11553669|qid=Q11553669|1=Kawaguchi Sengen Shrine}} (Yamanashi Prefecture{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Minamitsuru County|1=UNKNOWN}}{{ill|lt=Fujikawaguchiko Town|Q1004231|qid=Q1004231|1=Fujikawaguchiko}}), {{ill|lt=Ichinomiya Sengen Shrine|Q11352589|qid=Q11352589|1=Ichinomiya Sengen Shrine}} (Yamanashi Prefecture {{ill|lt=Nishiyashiro District|Q1131467|qid=Q1131467|1=Nishiyatsushiro District, Yamanashi}}{{ill|lt=Ichikawa Misato Town|Q1204453|qid=Q1204453|1=Ichikawamisato}}). For a discussion of comparison, see "{{ill|lt=Sengen Shrine #Kai Province|Q3471785|qid=Q3471785|1=Sengen shrine}}". </ref>, which shows the depth of reverence for Asama no Kami.

==== From the Kamakura period to the Sengoku period ====
[[File:Fuji Sankei Mandara from Fujisan Hongu Sengen Taisha.png|thumb|200px|right|{{center|"Fuji Mandala in Color on Silk"<br />(Muromachi period, National Important Cultural Property)}}{{small|Mt. Fuji at the top, Sengen Shrine precincts at the bottom left. }}]]Since then, he has been revered by {{ill|lt=court nobles|Q1196276|qid=Q1196276|1=Kuge}} and {{ill|lt=samurai|Q2927809|qid=Q2927809|1=Buke}}, and {{ill|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo|Q313942|qid=Q313942|1=Emperor Go-Daigo}}'s land was donated<ref group="hara">September 1334. Kanto Edition Volume 1, Document No. 141) In addition to this, samurai families repeatedly donated and restored shrine territories. In the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|qid=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}, {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo|Q242800|qid=Q242800|1=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} donated the shrine territory and {{ill|lt=Yoshitoki Hojo|Q1028811|qid=Q1028811|1=Hōjō Yoshitoki}} built the shrine. He was revered by the powerful figures of the time, such as group="Hara">``Hōjo Kudaiki'' Volume 4</ref>. According to the shrine biography (Fuji Hongu Sengenshaki), the origin of Yabusame at Sengen Taisha Shrine is that Minamoto no Yoritomo dedicated a Yabusame horse to him when he performed Maki-kari at Mt. group="commentary">The first look at the document is the "Takeda Family Red Seal" dated December 17, 1995 (Document No. 2238, "{{ill|lt=Sengoku Imon|Q11496033|qid=Q11496033|1=Sengoku Imon}}{{ill|lt=Takeda clan|Q1210114|qid=Q1210114|1=Takeda clan}}, Volume 3"). </ref>. {{ill|lt=Nanbokucho Period (Japan)|Q264012|qid=Q264012|lt=Nanbokucho Period|1=Nanboku-chō period}} {{ill|lt=Takauji Ashikaga|Q297107|qid=Q297107|1=Ashikaga Takauji}}<ref group="Hara">September 24, Kenmu 2, "Takauji Ashikaga Donation Letter" ("Nanbokucho Imon, Kanto Edition") Donation of shrine territory by {{ill|lt=Tadayoshi Ashikaga|Q725375|qid=Q725375|1=Ashikaga Tadayoshi}}<ref group="Hara">November 10, Kenmu 2nd year ``Letter of Donation'' by Tadayoshi Ashikaga (``Nanbokucho Imon Kanto Edition, Volume 1'' document No. 329)</ref>, {{ill|lt=Nori Imagawa|Q11376940|qid=Q11376940|1=Imagawa Noriuji}}<ref> group="Hara">January 11, 2016, "Written by Noriyoshi Imagawa" ("Imon of the Northern and Southern Courts, Kanto Edition, Volume 4, Document No. 2989") group="Hara">November 18, 1970, "Imagawa Noritaka (Yasunori) Letter of Donation" ("Muromachi Ibun Kanto Edition Volume 1" Document No. 680)</ref>. {{ill|lt=Takeda Shingen|Q276404|qid=Q276404|1=Takeda Shingen}} offered a letter of request<ref group="Hara">April 23, 1570, ``Takeda Shingen Ganmon'' (``Sengoku Imon Takeda Clan Edition'') Volume 3'' Document No. 1544</ref> After that, {{ill|lt=Katsuyori Takeda|Q662482|qid=Q662482|1=Takeda Katsuyori}} started construction from 4th year of Tensho and carried out the transfer of the shrine in 1578.<r ef>``Fuji Omiya Sengu Irimono Hikizaku'' says, ``Fuji Omiya Sengu Nyumono Hikizaku, the record of the establishment of Tensho Rokubo Tora Katsuyori in the 12th month''</ref><ref>{{ill|lt=Yu Hirayama (Historian)|Q11482490|qid=Q11482490|lt=Masaru Hirayama|1=Masaru Hirayama}}, ``Suruga Fuji Omiya Sengen Shrine Shrine Dedication Record'', Takeda Clan Research No. 45, 2012</ref>. group="Hara">"Toyotomi Hideyoshi Shuinchosha", December 1999 ("Asama Bunsho" P139)</ref>.

==== Edo period ====In the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, {{ill|lt=Ieyasu Tokugawa|Q171977|qid=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} not only secured 867 koku of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=shuinji|1=UNKNOWN}}, but also built the current shrine building to commemorate the victory at {{ill|lt=Battle of Sekigahara|Q234188|qid=Q234188|1=Battle of Sekigahara}}. In {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|qid=Q1328185|1=Keichō}}14 ({{ill|lt=1609|Q6762|qid=Q6762|1=1609}}), he obtained priority rights to acquire sansen at the summit of Mt. Fuji<ref name="suisenshogennan" />. Subsequent shoguns also donated prayer fees and repair fees, and the fourth shogun {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ietsuna|Q323422|qid=Q323422|1=Tokugawa Ietsuna}} donated 1,000 ryo of gold.<ref>{{jal ink|#Miyaji (1929)||Miyaji (1929)}}P58</ref>, the 5th Shogun {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Tsunayoshi|Q313745|qid=Q313745|1=Tokugawa Tsunayoshi}} has 50 pieces of silver and 2,000 pieces of gold. Ryo later donated 700 ryo of gold. #Miyaji (1929)||Miyaji (1929)}}P230</ref>, the 10th Shogun {{ill|lt=Ieharu Tokugawa|Q437518|qid=Q437518|1=Tokugawa Ieharu}} donated 300 pieces of silver. Even after that, the veneration of the Tokugawa family by successive shoguns never ceased. In addition, prayers were sometimes ordered by the shogunate, and in 1707, 100 pieces of silver were given for the Mt. (58)”, 2018</ref>. In {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Yasunaga|1=UNKNOWN}}8 ({{ill|lt=1779|Q7714|qid=Q7714|1=1779}}), the 8th station and above of Mt. Fuji were donated to Sengen Taisha Shrine with the approval of the {{ill|lt=three magistrates|Q11354845|qid=Q11354845|1=San-bugyō}} (see {{ill|lt=Mt. Fuji (representative topics)#Concession disputes over Mt.|UNKNOWN|lt=Refer to ``Concession disputes over Mt. Fuji''}})."Fugaku no Ki" ({{ill|lt=1733|Q6972|qid=Q6972|1=1733}}) describes the state of Sengen Taisha Shrine as follows: ``This is Sengen-ya at the base of Mt. Fuji, which enshrines Konohana Sakuyahime, the chief priest, Daiguji, twenty temples of shrine priests, a large number of cherry blossoms in the precincts, a shrine dedicated to the love of the gods, and a sanctuary to the east of the shrine.''

*{{ill|lt=1870|Q7741|qid=Q7741|1=1870}} (Meiji 3) - Later, {{ill|lt=Han Shishino|Q6536362|qid=Q6536362|1=Shishino Nakaba}}, who founded {{ill|lt=Fusokyo|Q11497295|qid=Q11497295|1=Fuso-kyo}}, became the first government-elected chief priest (he also served as the chief priest of {{ill|lt=Kitaguchi Hongu Fuji Sengen Shrine|Q11401286|qid=Q11401286|1=Kitaguchi Hongū Fuji Sengen Shrine}}).*{{ill|lt=1871|Q7746|qid=Q7746|1=1871}} (Meiji 4) {{ill|lt=May 14|Q2561|qid=Q2561|1=May 14}} - Added to {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kokuhei Chusha|1=UNKNOWN}} as ``''Sengen Shrine'''' under {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|qid=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}.
*{{ill|lt=1896|Q7846|qid=Q7846|1=1896}} (Meiji 29) {{ill|lt=July 8|Q2692|qid=Q2692|1=July 8}} - Promoted to {{ill|lt=Modern shrine rank system #government shrine|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=Kanpei Taisha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}.
*{{ill|lt=1907|Q2048|qid=Q2048|1=1907}} (Meiji 40) {{ill|lt=May 27|Q2587|qid=Q2587|1=May 27}} - Designated as a special protected building under the Ancient Shrines and Temples Preservation Act.
*{{ill|lt=1934|Q18714|qid=Q18714|1=1934}} (Showa 9) {{ill|lt=June 15|Q2613|qid=Q2613|1=June 15}} - The large torii gate was built in front of Fujinomiya Station.
*After {{ill|lt=World War II|Q362|qid=Q362|1=World War II}}, it was added to {{ill|lt=Beppyō Shrine|Q10898274|qid=Q10898274|1=Beppyo shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Shrine Honcho|Q908077|qid=Q908077|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}.
*{{ill|lt=1981|Q2437|qid=Q2437|1=1981}} (1981) {{ill|lt=March 27|Q2457|qid=Q2457|1=March 27}} - The large torii gate of Sengen Taisha Shrine in front of Fujinomiya Station was removed in the name of Gakunan regional city planning. <ref>[http://www.city.fujinomiya.shizuoka.jp/koho/shiseigaiyou/images/p6.pdf City history]</ref>*{{ill|lt=1982|Q2436|qid=Q2436|1=1982}} (1982) {{ill|lt=March 11|Q2398|qid=Q2398|1=March 11}} - In order to make the name appropriate for the main shrine of all Sengen shrines in Japan, the name was changed from ``Fujisan Hongu Sengen Shrine'' to the current ``''Fujisan Hongu Sengen Taisha''''.
*{{ill|lt=2006|Q2021|qid=Q2021|1=2006}} (Heisei 18) {{ill|lt=October 29th|Q2951|qid=Q2951|1=October 29}} - 1200th anniversary of the enshrinement is held. The Otorii was rebuilt in time for the event.<ref group="notes">Due to the strong demand for the Otorii to be revived, the 16m high Torii was relocated to the Kanda River, about 500m west of Fujinomiya Station, and rebuilt. </ref>.
*{{ill|lt=2013|Q1998|qid=Q1998|1=2013}} (2013) {{ill|lt=June 22|Q2663|qid=Q2663|1=June 22}} - Registered as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=World Cultural Heritage Site|1=UNKNOWN}} (13th cultural heritage site in Japan) as part of the component assets of "{{ill|lt=Mt. Fuji - Object of Faith and Source of Art|Q16482294|qid=Q16482294|1=Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration}}".*{{ill|lt=2020 Tokyo Olympics|Q181278|qid=Q181278|1=2020 Summer Olympics}} The venue served as a celebration venue for the torch relay, and approximately 10,000 torchbearers were selected through public recruitment. Regarding the torch relay, {{ill|lt=Tokyo Organizing Committee of the Olympic and Paralympic Games|Q15882575|qid=Q15882575|lt=Organizing Committee|1=Tokyo Organising Committee of the Olympic and Paralympic Games}} announced that a total of 535,717 applications were received for the runner recruitment conducted by four sponsor companies and each prefectural executive committee.<ref name="nikkei20190927">{{Cite news|title=Torch relay, 530,000 applications in total|newspaper=Nihon Keizai Shimbun|date=2019-09-27|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO50278340X20C19A9CR0000/|publication-date=|accessdate= September 27, 2019|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20190927125733/https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXMZO50278340X20C19A9CR0000/|archivedate=September 27, 2019}}</ref>.

=== Precincts ===

=== Motomiya ===

==== Shrine ====
[[File:Fujisanhongū-sengen-taisha honden.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Main shrine (National Important Cultural Property)}}{{small|It was built by Tokugawa Ieyasu and is called "{{ill|lt=Asama-zukuri|Q11557481|qid=Q11557481|1=Sengen-zukuri}}." }}]]The shrine building was built by Tokugawa Ieyasu in {{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|qid=Q1328185|1=Keichō}}9 ({{ill|lt=1604|Q6738|qid=Q6738|1=1604}}). {{ill|lt=Hoei Earthquake|Q2001581|qid=Q2001581|1=1707 Hōei earthquake}} ({{ill|lt=Hoei|Q1145262|qid=Q1145262|1=Hōei}}4 ({{ill|lt=1707|Q6841|qid=Q6841|1=1707}})) and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ansei Tokai Earthquake|1=UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Kaei|Q1146125|qid=Q1146125|1=Kaei}}November 4, 7 ({{ill|lt=1854|Q7670|qid=Q7670|1=1854}})) Some buildings collapsed, but the main shrine, worship hall, and tower gate still exist. Regarding the Ansei-Tokai Earthquake, ``The Great Earthquake Causes Great Damage to the Shrine'' was written, which describes the damage caused <ref>宮地直一、広野三郎 著、浅間神社 編『浅間神社の歴史』今古書院〈富士の研究〉、1929年。P244</ref>. Construction was attempted during the Muromachi period as well, and the construction costs covered the various functions of Fuji upper and lower Fuji.<ref>宮地直一、広野三郎 著、浅間神社 編『浅間神社の歴史』今古書院〈富士の研究〉、1929年。 P204</ref>, it seems that construction did not proceed smoothly in the war-torn world <ref>宮地直一、広野三郎 著、浅間神社 編『浅間神社の歴史』今古書院〈富士の研究〉、1929年。P205</ref>. It is often damaged due to wars, for example, the shrine buildings that were damaged during the {{ill|lt=Kawato Rebellion|Q11553961|qid=Q11553961|1=Katong Rebellion}} are being built. group="Hara">August 9, Eiroku 3, ``Imagawa clan authentic article'' (``Sengoku Ibun Imagawa clan edition, Volume 2'' document No. 1567)</ref>. Also, at this time, the company members ``Kiyonaga'' (Ichiwa Sho-shi) and ``Harunaga'' (Shiwa Sho-shi) were leading construction-related matters.<ref name="miyazaki">{{ill|lt=Makoto Higashijima|Q11526782|qid=Q11526782|1=Makoto Higashijima}}, "The premise of the public view of taxation: Demagnetization of solicitation," Historical Creation of the Public Sphere: Toward Ehu's Thought, P79-87, University of Tokyo Press, 2000</ref>.The main shrine is a nationally important cultural property, and has an unusual structure with a 5-ken row of girders, 4-length beams, and a hipped-style shrine building topped by a Sangen-style shrine building with a double tower structure. The roof is {{ill|lt=cypress bark|Q90373477|qid=Q90373477|1=cypress bark roof}}, and the characteristic form of this main hall is called ``''{{ill|lt=Asama-zukuri|Q11557481|qid=Q11557481|1=Sengen-zukuri}}'''.
[[File:Honden kamon.JPG|thumb|250px|left|{{center|{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Chrysanthemum crest|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|lt=Paulownia crest|Q1101561|qid=Q1101561|lt=Gosan paulownia crest|1=Paulownia crest}}}}{{small|In addition, a combination of ``Chrysanthemum crest'' and ``Aoi crest'' is attached to the toad. }}]]The Aoi Crest and the Fuji family crest, the Palm Crest, are attached to various places.<ref name="#4">宮地直一、広野三郎 著、浅間神社 編『浅間神社の歴史』今古書院〈富士の研究〉、1929年。P214</ref><ref>宮地直一、広野三郎 著、浅間神社 編『浅間神社の歴史』今古書院〈富士の研究〉、1929年。P303</ref><ref>宮地直一、広野三郎 著、浅間神社 編『浅間神社の歴史』今古書院〈富士の研究〉、1929年。P305</ref>, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=chrysanthemum crest|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=hollyhocks crest|Q11353976|qid=Q11353976|1=Aoi mon}}, and {{ill|lt=paulownia crest|Q1101561|qid=Q1101561|lt=five-three paulownia crest|1=Paulownia crest}} are attached to the toad leg.<ref name="kamon">Yasunori Takebe, "Architectural form of the main hall of Sengen Taisha Shrine: Study of Sengen-zukuri 2 (history and design)," Tokai Branch Research Report Collection 36, 1998</ref>. A record from the Edo period called ``Futake no Ki'' states that ``The painted carvings are beautiful and have chrysanthemum crests,'' and in fact, decorations with chrysanthemum and aoi crests still exist today. Also, because Mt. Fuji is considered a sacred object, there are also decorations of Mt. Fuji<ref name="kamon" />. The hall of worship has a gabled front with a gabled structure and a gabled structure at the back, and has a cypress bark roof like the main shrine. Both the inside and outside are tan-lacquered.It is believed that these constructions came from Ieyasu's wish to fulfill his prayer for victory at the Battle of Sekigahara, and there is also a mention of this in the historical materials of 1988<ref group="Hara" name="tokugawa" />. It is also said that the {{ill|lt=Sengu|Q2271023|qid=Q2271023|lt=Seisengu|1=Sengū}} ceremony during this construction was a grand one, with as many as 182 people working alone.

In ancient times, there were shrines and priests and {{ill|lt=kōri|Q11425851|qid=Q11425851|1=kori}} places, and there was a form of {{ill|lt=syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism|Q2279088|qid=Q2279088|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}. Although it cannot be seen today, Buddhist structures such as the "three-storied pagoda" are also located within the precincts, and can be seen in the 1670 year Kanbun 10 (1670) layout plan of the shrine building. </ref>.

<gallery>
File:Fujisanhongū-sengen-taisha haiden.JPG|Haden of worship (Shizuoka Prefecture designated cultural property)
File:Fujisanhongū-sengen-taisha roumon.JPG|Romon (Shizuoka Prefecture designated cultural property)
</gallery>

==== Company collection ====
[[File:SengenTaisha ShingenZakura.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Shingen Cherry Blossoms (Takeda Shingen Hand-planted Weeping Cherry Blossoms II)}}]]
The shrine complex spreads out behind the main shrine and is called ``Kandateyama.'' In the lyrics of the song {{ill|lt=Ryūben|Q11658883|qid=Q11658883|1=Ryūben}} in ``{{ill|lt=Zokugo Sentai Wakashu|Q1143277|qid=Q1143277|1=Shokugosen Wakashū}}'', there is a phrase ``April's Eve''. "If you can see the cherry blossoms in full bloom, you can read and attend to them if you seclude yourself at the shrine of Mt. Fuji in Suruga," which shows how many cherry blossom trees have been standing since ancient times. The shrine has a deep relationship with cherry blossoms due to the deity of the enshrined deity Konohananosakuyahime, and many cherry blossom trees are planted within the precincts. In front of the shrine, there were seven cherry blossom trees that are said to have been hand-planted by Takeda Shingen. Currently, the second generation of these ``Shingen cherry blossoms'' has been passed down within the shrine grounds.In addition, "Fuji Hongu Misaki" records that many trees were planted within the shrine by Katsuyori Takeda <ref>宮地直一、広野三郎 著、浅間神社 編『浅間神社の歴史』今古書院〈富士の研究〉、1929年。P213</ref>. It is said that there was something called ``Mannen Sugi'' in ancient times, and it is said to be the same as the ``Osugi'' that appears in ``{{ill|lt=Koyo Gunkan|Q586111|qid=Q586111|1=Kōyō Gunkan}}'' which says, ``Since the year of the rabbit, the cedar of Omiya in Suruga has been seen rising with smoke.''<ref name="#5"/> In addition, in the "Toyou Nikki" written by {{ill|lt=Hakura Kando|Q6564207|qid=Q6564207|1=Hakura Kandō}}, which was established in {{ill|lt=Tenpo|Q1369045|qid=Q1369045|1=Tenpō}}9 ({{ill|lt=1838|Q7612|qid=Q7612|1=1838}}), there is an old cedar tree with a circumference of 17m to the north of the shrine building. "Koyo Gunkan Fuji old cedar raw smoke Koreya," and this cedar is said to be the smoke-emitting old cedar described in the Koyo military guide.<ref>Takuya Inoue, "Mount Fuji Omiya-Murayama Exit Climb Route in the Early Modern Period as Seen in Climbing Records," Mt. Fuji Kaguyahime Museum Bulletin No. 32, 2017.</ref>. "Suruga Province Shinfudoki" records that this Mannensugi later withered and died.

==== Gomado ====
There used to be a Goma-do hall within the precincts. This Goma-do Hall, like the main shrine building, was damaged during the Kato Rebellion, and the Imagawa clan ordered its reconstruction through solicitation. However, ``Hōjō-in'', which is the head shrine's head shrine, built it on its own, and the Imagawa clan donated the repaired portion to Hōjō-in, deeming it ``sacred and miraculous.''<ref group="Hara">October 26, 1987, ``Imagawa Clan Authentic Articles'' (Sengoku Ibun, edited by the Imagawa clan) Volume 2, Document No. 1598)</ref><ref>Hiromichi Hasegawa, “Temple and Shrine Protection Policy of the Sengoku Daimyo Imagawa Clan – Focusing on Construction,” Shigaku Kenkyūroku 16, 1991</ref><ref>Yasumasa Otaka, Mt. Fuji Worship and Shugendo, pp. 325-326, Iwata Shoin, 2013.

In recent years, excavations have progressed, and during excavations in {{ill|lt=2008|Q2004|qid=Q2004|1=2008}} (Heisei 20), the remains of the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Goma-do|1=UNKNOWN}} building, which is shown in the shrine layout plan (supposed to have been created in {{ill|lt=1670|Q7606|qid=Q7606|1=1670}}), were confirmed on the north side of Wakutama Pond. It is said to be one of the materials for the syncretization of Shintoism and Buddhism, and research has shown that it was located in a position where climbers of Mt. Fuji could look down on Goma-do.<ref>Remains of Goma-do building confirmed; excavation site on the grounds of Mt. Fuji Hongu Sengen Taisha Shrine opened to the public (Shizuoka Shimbun) 2008/07/16</ref> In addition, items related to celadon bowls, white porcelain pots, and blue-and-white porcelain were unearthed, as well as cobblestone pavement along the road at the site of the Goma-do Hall, and the remains of medieval stone collection structures.

==== Others ====
In front of the tower gate, there is a ``Sakura no Baba'' that stretches from east to west, and is used for rituals such as the Yabusame ceremony. There is also Kagami Pond, also known as Spectacle Pond.

On the east side is {{ill|lt=Wakutama Pond|Q11563392|qid=Q11563392|1=Wakutamaike}}, which is made up of spring water from Mt. Fuji that gushes out within the temple grounds. It gushes out from between many layers of {{ill|lt=lava|Q40157|qid=Q40157|1=Lava}}, and is designated as a {{ill|lt=natural monument #special natural monument|Q23790|qid=Q23790|lt=special natural monument|1=Natural monument}}. The vermilion-painted Mizuya Shrine is located on the rock at the water source.

As Sengen Taisha Shrine is located at the starting point of the {{ill|lt=Omiya-Murayama-guchi Ascending Route|Q11455992|qid=Q11455992|1=Ōmiya-Murayama Ascending Route}}, there was a Dojabo temple for pilgrims to Mt. Fuji, and the shrine's people provided lodging for pilgrims to climb Mt. Fuji. This is called ``Omiya Dojabo''. ``Omiya Dojabo Kimon'' states, ``The story of Omiya Dojabo, from ancient times to the Kyoroku and Tenbun eras, there were more than 30 temples.'' It is known that there were more than 30 Dojabo in the late Muromachi period.<ref name="ootaka">Yasumasa Otaka, "Examination of the Fuji Pilgrimage Mandala - Focusing on books owned by the Fujisan Hongu Sengen Taisha Shrine and designated by the government," Fujisan Faith and Shugendo, 2013</ref>.

The successive Antarctic research vessels ``{{ill|lt=Soya (ship)|Q138537|qid=Q138537|lt=Soya|1=Sōya (PL107)}}'', ``Fuji'', and ``{{ill|lt=Shirase (icebreaker, first generation)|Q6158738|qid=Q6158738|lt=Shirase|1=Japanese icebreaker Shirase (AGB-5002)}}'' have shrines installed, and are used to worship and pray at Sengen Taisha Shrine. </ref>

=== Okumiya ===
[[File:Okumiya (Sengen-taisha).JPG|thumb|250x250px|{{center|Okumiya}}]]
[[File:sengen_kengamine.jpg|thumb|250x250px|{{center|Top of Mt. Fuji}}{{small|Okumiya (red roof) in the center. Mt. Mishima on the left, [[Mt. Kengamine (Mt. Fuji)|Mt. Kengamine]] on the right. }}]]The Okumiya at the top of Mt. Fuji is located at the top of {{ill|lt=Omiya/Murayama Exit Climbing Route|Q11455992|qid=Q11455992|1=Ōmiya-Murayama Ascending Route}}. The entire precincts of Okumiya are designated as a ``Special Protection Area'' in the Mt. Fuji region of {{ill|lt=Fuji-Hakone-Izu National Park|Q1137542|qid=Q1137542|1=Fuji-Hakone-Izu National Park}}. It was originally Dainichi-do, which formed {{ill|lt=Fujisan Koho-ji Temple|Q11455997|qid=Q11455997|1=Fujisan Kōbō-ji Temple}}, but due to the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ordinance to Separate Shinto and Buddhism|1=UNKNOWN}}, the Buddhist statues were removed and the site was managed as Sengen Taisha Okumiya.<ref name="#6">宮地直一、広野三郎 著、浅間神社 編『浅間神社の歴史』今古書院〈富士の研究〉、1929年。P27</ref><ref name="fujisan">{{PDFlink | It was customary for Dainichi-do to be called ``Omote-Dainichi,'' and Yakushi-do to be called ``Urayakushi''. </ref>.A stone monument with the words ``Mt. Fuji Summit Asama Taisha Okumiya'' has been erected within the Okumiya precincts, and it is a symbol of the mountaintop. The Yakushi-do Hall at the top of the mountain served as a collection point for Yamayakusen, but due to the Haibutsu Kishaku movement, it became a subsidiary shrine of Sengen Taisha and was managed as {{ill|lt=Kusushi Shrine (Mt. Fuji)|Q11369789|qid=Q11369789|lt=Kusushi Shrine|1=Kusushi Shrine}} (Tohoku Okumiya).<ref name="#6"/><ref>宮地直一、広野三郎 著、浅間神社 編『浅間神社の歴史』今古書院〈富士の研究〉、1929年。P331-338</ref>.

On the door of the inner shrine of Sengen Taisha Shrine, there is a large golden inscription that says ``Kokushinmujodake,'' and there is an ``Elderly Person Registration Office'' inside the building. The {{ill|lt=opening festival|Q11655214|qid=Q11655214|1=opening festival}} will be held on July 11th, and a Shinto priest will be on hand until the end of August to perform rituals and award amulets. Okumiya's regular festival is held on August 15th. After the closing ceremony in September, the mountain remains uninhabited until the following year when the mountain opens.

Due to the influence of the {{ill|lt=new coronavirus infection_(2019)|Q84263196|qid=Q84263196|lt=new coronavirus|1=COVID-19}}, the 2020 regular festival will be held at a mountain hut etc. The mountain trail was closed to prevent the spread of infection due to the Three Cs}}, and the Hongu Shrine Opening Festival was held on July 15th, the Okumiya Opening Festival on the 31st, and the Hongu and Okumiya Closing Festival on August 15th, attended only by those involved. In modern times, the only time that the annual festival was not held at Okumiya was in {{ill|lt=1945|Q5240|qid=Q5240|1=1945}}, and on that occasion, the annual festival was held at the 1st station. <ref>Shinja Shimpo September 21, 2020 p.3 </ref>

==== History of Okumiya precincts ====
During the Edo period, under the patronage of Tokugawa Ieyasu, Sengen Taisha Shrine came to manage and control the mountaintop, based on the fact that it had priority rights in building the main shrine and acquiring Naiin Sansen.<ref name="suisenshogennan" /><ref group="Hara" name="tokugawa" group="Hara">Yasunei 8 (1779) "Shogunate Letter of Approval" ("Asama Documents")</ref>. In 1779, the shogunate officially granted control over the 8th station and above<ref name="suisenshogennan" /><ref group="Hara" name="tokugawa" />, which continues to this day. The section ``Upper Mt. Fuji'' in ``Suruga Province Shinfudoki'' (Edo period topography) states that ``Above the 8th station is the place where Omiya Sengen Daiguji Betto was located in Fuji County, and the details appear to be in a public court document handed down on December 5, 2017.''The land donated to Sengen Taisha Shrine was nationalized for a time. According to the {{ill|lt=National Property Law|Q11420951|qid=Q11420951|1=National Government Asset Act}}'s ``Law Concerning the Disposal of National Property Loaned Free of Charge to Shrines and Temples,'' the land of temples and shrines across the country was returned free of charge from the government. As Sengen Taisha Shrine, whose sacred mountain is Mt. Fuji, had been managing donated land for a long time, the same law should have been applied to it like other temples and shrines, but because it was a special mountain, it was not applied as an exception<ref name="okunomiya" />. Originally, according to the law, the land should have been treated as belonging to the shrine, but only 49,952 tsubo (49,952 tsubo) was transferred. In response, Sengen Taisha filed a petition<ref name="okunomiya" />.

Based on conclusive evidence such as ancient documents showing that the Edo shogunate had donated to Sengen Shrine, it was once again confirmed through a court case that these lands were within the precincts of Sengen Taisha Shrine. Based on this lawsuit, the land was returned to the Sengen Taisha Shrine in 2004.<ref name="suisenshogennan" /><ref group="notes">The area above the 8th station is approximately 4 million m{{sup|2}}, excluding mountain trails and {{ill|lt=Mt. Fuji Weather Observatory|Q11455995|qid=Q11455995|lt=former Mt. Fuji Weather Observatory|1=Mount Fuji Weather Station}}. </ref>. However, the land has not been registered because the {{ill|lt=prefectural border|Q11582641|qid=Q11582641|1=prefectural border}} between Shizuoka Prefecture and {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|qid=Q132720|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}} has not been determined.

==== Mountaintop worship ruins ====
In ancient times, it was believed that the closer you got to the top of the mountain, the more sacred it became. For this reason, donations and dedications were made repeatedly to the mountaintop, and as a result, the current mountaintop worship ruins were formed<ref name="suisenshogennan" />. The first religious facility on the mountaintop was the facility (later Dainichi-do) built by {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Late Shonin|1=UNKNOWN}}, where scriptures and Buddhist statues were dedicated. Also, giving money to Naiin (crater) was an act of worshiping the deity and Buddha said to be enshrined in Naiin <ref name="suisenshogennan" />.

Dedications were held repeatedly in this way, and there were many Buddhist statues that were part of religious ruins on the mountaintop. However, many of them were removed due to {{ill|lt=Haibutsu Kishaku|Q702298|qid=Q702298|1=Haibutsu kishaku}}, and only a few remain today.Also, at the top of the mountain, there is a rock called ``Tiger Rock'' because the rock that juts out into the crater looks like a tiger. There is a "Toraiwa Monument" nearby.<ref>[http://www.fuji-hongu.or.jp/sengen/okumiya/#top Mt. Fuji summit Sengen Taisha Okumiya (Mt. Fuji Hongu Sengen Taisha official website)] <ref>宮地直一、広野三郎 著、浅間神社 編『浅間神社の歴史』今古書院〈富士の研究〉、1929年。P335</ref>.

==== Okumiya's red seal, etc. ====
The {{ill|lt=shuin (shrines and temples)|Q7504772|qid=Q7504772|lt=shuin|1=Shuin}} are also different between the main shrine and the inner shrine. The goshuin stamp is specially made and contains sand from the lava of Mt. Fuji.

If you register on the ``elderly pilgrim list'' at the elderly person registration office (if you are 70 years of age or older), you will be given a commemorative gift. </ref>. It is handled at Okumiya and Kusushi Shrine, the terminal shrine of Sengen Taisha Shrine, and the cumulative number of people as of 2010 is 1,243 <ref>Asahi Shimbun, October 26, 2010</ref>.

[[File:Yamamiya-sengen-jinja iseki-2.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{center|{{ill|lt=Yamamiya Sengen Shrine|Q11467749|qid=Q11467749|1=Yamamiya Sengen Shrine}} (Motomiya)}}]]

Later, in the first year of {{ill|lt=Daido (Japan)|Q1373191|qid=Q1373191|lt=Daido|1=Daidō}} ({{ill|lt=806|Q30021|qid=Q30021|1=806}}), Sengen-kami is said to have moved to Omiya, but even after that, this area remained deeply involved in the rituals of Sengen Taisha Shrine as the main shrine and mountain shrine.
*: This Yamamiya Shrine does not have a main shrine, and retains its ancient ritual form as a place of worship from a distance of Mt. Fuji.

=== Affiliated companies ===
The current Sessusue Shrine has seven shrines within its precincts as mentioned above under Sessha Bushi and Sessha Bushi, but in ancient times there were many Sessusue Shrines within and outside the precincts (for details, see {{ill|lt=#Changes of Sessusue Shrines|UNKNOWN|lt=Changes of Sessusue Shrines}}). In particular, the final shrines of {{ill|UNKNOWN|qid=Q134917286|lt=Shikiuchi|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286|lt=Shikinaisha}} were {{ill|lt=Fuchi Shrine|Q11457275|qid=Q11457275|1=Fukuchi Shrine}} and {{ill|lt=Wabun Shrine (Fujinomiya City)|Q11387089|qid=Q11387089|lt=Wabun Shrine|1=Shidori Shrine (Fujinomiya)}}.

In addition, the ``Suruga Province Shinto Name Book'' lists deities related to Sengen Taisha Shrine from the 1st Miko Myojin to the 18th Miko Myojin under the name ``Asama Miko Myojin''. Of these, the Third Mikogami and the Seventh Mikogami are enshrined as separate shrines within the grounds of the main shrine. Outside the precincts, the First Mikogami is enshrined at Wakanomiya Sengen Shrine, and the second Mikogami at Ninomiya Sengen Shrine is enshrined to the north of the main shrine, and there is a theory that the Eighth Mikogami and the Eighteenth Mikogami are enshrined at {{ill|lt=Yonenomiya Sengen Shrine|Q11603971|qid=Q11603971|1=Yonenomiya Sengen Shrine}}, and each was a separate shrine of Sengen Taisha Shrine in ancient times. Furthermore, the whereabouts of the other Mikogami are not known.

=== Changes in Setsusha ===
Changes of Setsusue Shrine since the Edo period<ref>The changes of Setsusue Shrine are described based on "History of Sengen Shrine" Chapter 11, Setsusue Shrine. </ref>.
<div class="NavFrame" style="width:100%;">
 <div class="NavHead" style="padding:1.5px; line-height:1.7; letter-spacing:1px;">Sessuesha History</div>
 <div class="NavContent" style="text-align:left;">
All company names are written in {{ill|lt=common kanji|Q875368|qid=Q875368|1=Jōyō kanji}}.
'''Edo period'''
* Setsusha: 19 companies (all are Setsusha outside the boundaries of the prefecture)
** Wakamiya Hachimangu Shrine, {{ill|lt=Kinnomiya|Q128867078|qid=Q128867078|1=Kannomiya Shrine}}, Fukuishi Myojin Shrine, Gozu Tenno, Sanno Gongen, Sansha Myojin, {{ill|lt=Tosho Shrine (Fujinomiya City)|Q134888235|qid=Q134888235|lt=Our Daimyojin Shrine|1=Tōsho Shrine (Fujinomiya City)}}, Fukunomiya, Kifune Myojin, Yonenomiya Sengen Shrine, Wakanomiya, Ninomiya, Kosengen Shrine, Yamamiya, Fuji Shrine, Wabun Shrine, Akioji Daimyojin, Akioji Inari, Kotohira/Yamagami/Akiba Goshu Shrine
* Sub-shrines: 15 (all sub-shrines within the precincts)
** Sannomiya Shrine, Shichinomiya Shrine, Kojin Shrine, Mizuya Myojin, Mizu Shrine, Benten Shrine, Gozu Tenno Shrine, Hinomiya Shrine, Ise Shrine, Hachiman Shrine, Ten Shrine, Benten Shrine, Additional Myojin Shrine, Mime Shrine, Iizake Oji Shrine*: Ten shrines, Ara Shrine, Mizu Shrine, Gozu Tenno Shrine, Hinomiya Shrine, Ise Shrine, Hachiman Shrine, Benten Shrine, Additional Myojin Shrine, Mime Shrine, and Iizake Oji Shrine, were destroyed in the Great Ansei Earthquake and were enshrined together in the inner shrine. Although the name can be seen from the Meiji period onward, all of them are subsidiary shrines with the inner shrine enshrined together.

'''After Meiji'''
: Changes in Setsusha Shrine since the Meiji period. The literature is as follows.
* Meiji 4: Meiji 4 (1871) "Shikiuchi Sengen Shrine Giant Investigation Report Book"
* 8th year of the Meiji era (1875): "Kokuhei Chusha Sengen Shrine Setsu-subshu-sha table" submitted to the Shizuoka prefectural government in 1875
* Meiji 11: “Sessuesha Interrogation Record” revised in November 1878
*Currently: “History of Sengen Shrine” (Konko Shoin)

=== Yamamiya Mikamiyuki ===
[[File:Goshinko.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|Goshinko Michi's Headmark}}]]
An event called ``Yamamiya Sengen Shrine #Yamamiya Gojinko'' was held between Yamamiya Sengen Shrine and Sengen Taisha Shrine (for details, see ``Yamamiya Sengen Shrine #Yamamiya Gojinko | Yamamiya Gojinko''). This is an event that involves going back and forth between Sengen Taisha Shrine and Yamamiya Sengen Shrine, and although it is known from literature that it was already being held in 1577 (``Fuji Omiya Goshinjicho''), the exact year it started is unknown. It continued until {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|qid=Q7757|1=1873}}.

The interpretation of this ritual is that the god returns to the historic site (Yamiya) in April, or that the mountain god descends to the village (Omiya) as the god of rice fields in April<ref name="suisenshogennan" />.A picture of the head of the shrine in 1670 depicts the Shinko Bridge<ref name="ootaka" />, which was used during the shrine visit to the shrine. Local people were prohibited from passing through it, and it is said that it was made of vermillion-lacquered pillars with a cypress bark roof.<ref>宮地直一、広野三郎 著、浅間神社 編『浅間神社の歴史』今古書院〈富士の研究〉、1929年。P304</ref>. The route used for Yamamiya Goshinko is called ``Oshinkomichi,'' and its starting point is Shinko Bridge. A signpost for the path to the shrine was found in the ground in the precincts in 1984 (Showa 59), and it was written as ``November, the end of the fourth year of Genroku'', indicating that it was dedicated in the fourth year of Genroku ({{ill|lt=1691|Q7682|qid=Q7682|1=1691}})<ref>Ayuko Saito, ``Essay on the Restoration of the Path to the Shrine of the Yamamiya Shrine'' The road connecting Fujisan Hongu Sengen Taisha and Yamamiya Sengen Shrine,” Aspects of Places of Prayer, 2017 </ref>. Currently erected on the banks of Wakutama Pond, <ref>Joint Conference of the Two Prefectures to Promote the Registration of Mt. Fuji as a World Cultural Heritage Site, ``Mt. Fuji - Sacred place and source of artistic inspiration = World Heritage Site'' I-119, 2014 </ref>.

=== Cultural assets ===

=== Important cultural property (designated by the country) ===
* Main shrine (building)
*: Built in 1604 ({{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|qid=Q1328185|1=Keichō}}9). Designated on May 27, 1907 ({{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}40)<ref>The date of designation is stated with reference to [http://www.city.fujinomiya.shizuoka.jp/e-bunka/bunkazailiszt.pdf List of designated cultural properties in Fujinomiya City] (Fujinomiya City homepage) (viewed on July 10, 2013). </ref>.
* Colored Fuji Mandala on Silk (Painting)
*: Made in the Muromachi period. Designated on June 11, 1977.
* Tachi Meinan Muyakushi Ruriko Nyorai Bizen Province Nagafuneju {{ill|lt=Kagemitsu|Q11514646|qid=Q11514646|1=Kagemitsu}} (Craft)
*: Made in the Muromachi period. It is said to have been dedicated to Takeda Shingen. ``{{ill|lt=Shuko Jushu|Q11659296|qid=Q11659296|1=Shūko Jisshu}}'' has ``Illustration of the Takeda Shingen Tachi from Fuji Sengen Shrine Collection in Suruga Province'' <ref> Norihisa Kawami, ````Shuko Jushu'' Weapons Edition and 18th Century Ancient Weapon Research,'' p. 93, Kurokawa Ancient Culture Research Institute Bulletin of Ancient Culture Research No. 16, 2017</ref>. Designated on February 8, 1912 (Meiji 45).
* Wakizashi Inscription of Fuji Hongu Minamoto Shikibu Jo Shinkuni Ichigo Ikko o Eishi February 22nd <ref group="Notes">'22nd year' means '34th year'. The name of the sword is written as ``22'', avoiding the ``four'' character, which sounds like ``death''. </ref>(Crafts)*: Made in the Muromachi period. It is a sword made by the {{ill|lt=Shingoku school|Q11386011|qid=Q11386011|1=Nobukuni school}}. The inscription on the front of the finger reads ``Fuji Hongu Minamoto no Shikibu Nobukuni,'' and the inscription on the back of the finger indicates that it was made in 1427. It was a treasure of Sengen Taisha Shrine since ancient times, but it was lost during the War, and was later dedicated by {{ill|lt=Anayama Nobutomo|Q4752463|qid=Q4752463|1=Anayama Nobutomo}} on February 2, 1547.<ref group="Hara">{{ill|lt=Tenbun (Era name)|Q1192853|qid=Q1192853|lt=Tenbun|1=Tenbun}} February 2, 1547, ``Anayama Nobutomo Letter of Donation'' (``Sengoku Ibun, edited by the Imagawa clan, Volume 2'', No. 821)</ref>. A lacquered box was also included for the dedication, and the lid of the box reads, ``Dedication of Fuji Omiya Sengen Daibodhisattva's In-house Sword Ikko Sengenmaru.'' <ref group="Hara"> ``Anayama Shintomo Box Inscription'' ``Sengoku Ibun Imagawa Family Edition Volume 2'' [Reference 15] (P4)</ref>. Designated on February 8, 1912 (Meiji 45).

=== Shizuoka Prefecture designated cultural property ===
*Tangible cultural property
** Shrine building (building) - Designated on January 30, 1954.
*** Worship Hall, Heiden Hall, Transparent Wall - Constructed in 1604 ({{ill|lt=Keicho|Q1328185|qid=Q1328185|1=Keichō}}9).
*** Romon - Built in 1614 (Keicho 19).
** Fuji Sengen Mandala (Painting) - Designated on October 23, 1981.
** Large celadon vase with lotus petals (craft) - Designated on March 18, 1977.
** Celadon floating peony incense burner (craft) - Designated on March 18, 1977.
** Doll hand celadon large tea bowl (attached to Kuruinbori Tenmokudai) (craft) - Designated on March 18, 1977.
** Teppanyaki Koishi Igogomai Dogusoku (Craft)
**: It has a top body (Mogami-do) with an iron board plate (Itazane) and a red thread hair puller, and it is called a five-piece body because the five pieces on the front, back, and sides (the left side is divided into two pieces) are connected by a hinge. It is said to have been dedicated by Katsuyori Takeda. All the metal fittings are painted with gold nashiji, and the flower rhombus crest can be seen on the hassou studs on the chest plate.<ref>Ichiro Miura, ``Armor and Swords from the Collection of Sengen Taisha'', ``Armor and Swords of Takeda Shingen and Katsuyori'', Miyatai Publishing, 2011</ref>. The front of the helmet is modeled after Mt. Fuji, and is a distinctive decoration. Designated on March 18, 1977.
* Intangible folk cultural assets
** Fujinomiya Hayashi - Designated on March 20, 1995.

=== Cultural property designated by Fujinomiya City ===
[[File:Fujisanhongū-sengen-taisha baba.JPG|thumb|200px|{{center|Sakura Baba where Yabusame is held}}]]
*Tangible cultural property
** Large naginata by Yosuke Dengen (craft) - Designated on May 10, 1965 (Showa 40).
** Two accompanying statues (sculpture) - Designated on May 25, 1993.
** Emperor Goyozei's kanji (calligraphy/canonical books) - Designated on May 10, 1965 (Showa 40).
* Intangible folk cultural assets
** Mt.Fuji Hongu Sengen Taisha Yabusame - Designated on September 8, 2006.

=== Other cultural assets ===
* Arms
** Various Idomaru - According to the shrine's legend, it was dedicated to Takeda Shingen <ref>宮地直一、広野三郎 著、浅間神社 編『浅間神社の歴史』今古書院〈富士の研究〉、1929年。P714</ref>.
** Irisou Daisode Issou - Flower rhombus crest is used on the hardware, a decoration common to Irodomaru.
*Crafts
** Koto 2 Hari - Dedicated by Katsuyori Takeda at the time of Sengu <ref>宮地直一、広野三郎 著、浅間神社 編『浅間神社の歴史』今古書院〈富士の研究〉、1929年。P731</ref>.
* Books
** Mount Fuji - Written by {{ill|lt=Akinji Miura|Q11356455|qid=Q11356455|1=Miura Akitsugu}}<ref>宮地直一、広野三郎 著、浅間神社 編『浅間神社の歴史』今古書院〈富士の研究〉、1929年。P711</ref>.

=== Related historical materials ===
* “Suruga Fuji Omiya Sengen Shrine Shrine Horse Dedication Record”
*: A record of many Takeda vassals dedicating sacred horses when Takeda Katsuyori built Sengen Taisha Shrine during the Sengoku period. Although the original does not exist, a copy of the sacred horse offering letter signed by Nobutaka Takeda and others (Cabinet Bunko Collection "Tamao Bunko Documents"), {{ill|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|Q132720|qid=Q132720|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Yamanashi City|Q840610|qid=Q840610|1=Yamanashi (city)}}{{jalink|Eisho-in Temple in Yatsubo} (Yamanashi City)|Q11550100|Eisho-in Temple}} Manuscripts such as ``Kabutogan Shiryaku'' are known, and it is said that Tokushige Takano Inaba, a member of the Sengen Shrine family, served as {{ill|lt=intermediary (history)|Q11410664|qid=Q11410664|lt=intermediary|1=intermediary}} at the time of dedication. Quotations can be found here and there in the late Edo period topography ``{{ill|lt=Kokushi|Q11577871|qid=Q11577871|1=Kaikokushi}},'' but no manuscript of the ``Honoki'' itself has been recorded.
*: For the Kai Takeda clan, there is no existing {{ill|lt=minute book|Q11396171|qid=Q11396171|1=minute book}}, and this historical material is the ``Ikushima Ashijima Shrine opening letter'' dated 10th year of Eiroku ({{ill|lt=1567|Q6642|qid=Q6642|1=1567}}). '' and ``{{ill|lt=Tensho Jingo Kishomon|Q17218403|qid=Q17218403|1=Tensho Jingo Kishomon}}'' in the 10th year of the Tensho era ({{ill|lt=1582|Q6719|qid=Q6719|1=1582}}), it is regarded as a historical document that reveals the actual situation of Takeda vassals.<ref name="#7">Hirayama (2012), p.33</ref>, "Honoki" is considered important as a historical document that can provide a glimpse into the vassals after the {{ill|lt=Battle of Nagashino|Q428369|qid=Q428369|1=Battle of Nagashino}} in the 3rd year of Tensho ({{ill|lt=1575|Q6688|qid=Q6688|1=1575}}), which is between the two periods<ref name="#7"/>*: Hachiro Sato compiled the quotations recorded in the "Kokushi" and estimates that the ``Hodoniki'' was established in December 1992 ({{ill|lt=1578|Q6707|qid=Q6707|1=1578}}), the year Sengen Taisha was transferred.<ref>Sato (1992)</ref>. On the other hand, Masaru Hirayama considers the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=official position|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=recipient name|Q10912844|qid=Q10912844|1=Zuryou-mei}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=dogo|1=UNKNOWN}}, year of death, etc. of the Takeda vassals recorded in the Eishoin manuscript, and places the establishment between January and May of the 5th year of Tensho.
*: Hirayama also conducted a comparative study of the records in the ``Kokushi'' and the Eishoin version, and pointed out that the Eishoin version contained more names of vassals, and pointed out that the historical materials referenced when compiling the ``Kokushi'' may have been part of the whole <ref>Hirayama (2012), p. 32</ref>. On the other hand, since the records of "Kokushi" include people who are not registered in the Eishoin version, it is said that the Eishoin version was also part of the whole <ref>Hirayama (2012), pp.32-33</ref>.

=== Local information ===
'''Location'''
* Motomiya: {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|qid=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Fujinomiya City|Q496072|qid=Q496072|1=Fujinomiya, Shizuoka}}Miyamachi 1-1
* Okumiya: Awakura Jisaki, Fujinomiya City, Shizuoka Prefecture - Summit of Mt. Fuji. It is located at the end of the mountain trail from Fujinomiya Exit.

'''Access (to the main shrine)'''
* Railway
** Approximately 10 minutes walk (800m) from {{ill|lt=Tokai Railway|Q513679|qid=Q513679|1=Central Japan Railway Company}} (JR Tokai) {{ill|lt=Minobu Line|Q162747|qid=Q162747|1=Minobu Line}} {{ill|lt=Fujinomiya Station|Q5356094|qid=Q5356094|1=Fujinomiya Station}}
** 9 minutes (650m) from the same line {{ill|lt=Nishifujinomiya Station|Q7040330|qid=Q7040330|1=Nishi-Fujinomiya Station}}
* car
** About 15 minutes (6.5Km) from {{ill|lt=Shin-Tomei Expressway|Q875557|qid=Q875557|1=Shin-Tōmei Expressway}} {{ill|lt=Shin-Fuji Interchange|Q11082300|qid=Q11082300|lt=Shin-Fuji IC|1=Shin-Fuji interchange}} via {{ill|lt=Nishi-Fuji Road|Q11627678|qid=Q11627678|1=Nishi-Fuji Road}}
** About 20 minutes (9.5Km) from {{ill|lt=Tomei Expressway|Q1135694|qid=Q1135694|1=Tōmei Expressway}} {{ill|lt=Fuji Interchange|Q10954012|qid=Q10954012|lt=Fuji IC|1=Fuji interchange}} via Nishi-Fuji Road
*Highway bus
** Take the express bus {{ill|lt=Yakisoba Express|Q11280253|qid=Q11280253|1=Yakisoba Express}} between Tokyo Station and Fujinomiya and get off at Fujinomiya Station.
*Main route buses
** {{ill|lt=Fuji-Q Shizuoka Bus|Q11456063|qid=Q11456063|1=Fujikyu Shizuoka Bus}} From the north exit of Fujinomiya Station, take the Manno Awakura Loop Line for about 5 minutes and get off at Wakutama no Ike.** {{ill|lt=Miya Bus|Q11453140|qid=Q11453140|1=Miya Bus}} From the south exit of Fujinomiya Station, take the Miya 2, 13, and 14 lines for about 8 minutes and get off at Sengen Taisha-mae (buses 1 and 8 are suspended on Saturdays, Sundays, and holidays. Closed during the year-end and New Year holidays).
**: In addition, within the Fujikyu Shizuoka Bus "Tyokuro-kun" patrol route. Yamako Town Coach - Walk after getting off at Kanda Dori or Fujisan Sekaisan Center.

For information on getting to Okumiya, see {{ill|lt=Mountain Climbing|Q11456115|qid=Q11456115|1=mountaineering in Mount Fuji}}.

=== References ===

Please distinguish related documents that are not actually referred to but that are useful for further understanding this item by using a section name such as "Related Literature") -->
'''Original text'''
* "Fuji Hongu Sengenshaki"
*: A social biography written by the 41st head of the Fuji clan, Fuji (Wanibe) Tamizai, during the {{ill|lt=Kansei|Q1195131|qid=Q1195131|1=Kansei}} years ({{ill|lt=1789|Q7772|qid=Q7772|1=1789}}-{{ill|lt=1801|Q6877|qid=Q6877|1=1801}}). The text is included in "History of Sengen Shrine", pages 16-17, which will be posted later.
